prompt: A bank approved an AI loan-assessment tool at launch, then over eight months the vendor updated the model and the team added new data fields (age, postal code) without a re-review. Which statements correctly explain the governance failure? Select ALL that apply.
choices: The use was tiered once and never re-tiered, so newly added bias-prone fields went un-assessed
Treating governance as a one-time launch gate let unreviewed risk hide behind the old approval
Because every decision was audit-logged, the bank could still reconstruct which model version and fields drove each decision
The launch approval automatically covers any later model or data change, so no re-review was ever needed

prompt: A company makes its AI approval process so heavy that staff quietly use unsanctioned tools off the books. In trade-off terms, what has happened and how should it be addressed? Select ALL that apply.
choices: Risk reduction was over-weighted versus speed of adoption, producing 'shadow AI' that runs unrecorded
Shadow AI makes the AI use register go stale, so real exposure becomes invisible
Making the sanctioned path faster than the workaround helps bring uses back under governance
Shadow AI is harmless because staff are still getting work done

prompt: Why are audit logging and a human-in-the-loop record described as non-optional for high-tier, regulated AI uses? Select ALL that apply.
choices: When an AI-influenced decision is challenged, you must show who decided, on what basis, and with what oversight
They make the decision explainable and defensible to a regulator after the fact
A rubber-stamp review that records no real scrutiny still provides full accountability
Logging the model version and inputs lets you reconstruct how a specific decision was reached
